care_notes: Compact, low-spreading evergreen ideal for low hedges and underplanting. Trim once after the spring flush; tolerates shade and urban conditions.
Vigorous upright form that makes a tall, dense screen quickly. Cut by hand with secateurs to avoid shredding the large leaves.
The classic fast hedging laurel with broad rounded leaves. Prune in late spring and again in late summer to keep it dense.
Robust, frost-hardy selection with glossy large leaves for big hedges. One trim a year keeps it tidy in most gardens.
Slow, compact grower whose new growth emerges bronze-red before turning glossy green. Needs little pruning, perfect for smaller plots.
Narrow, columnar habit makes a slim space-saving hedge. Frost-hardy and disease-resistant; trim once in early summer.
Bushy, well-branched cultivar with medium leaves that clips neatly. Reliable and hardy for formal hedging.
Upright with narrow dark leaves, forming a fine-textured hedge that takes shaping well. Very hardy and shade-tolerant.
Low, horizontally spreading form with willow-like leaves; good ground cover on banks. Needs minimal pruning.
Dwarf compact selection with small leaves for low edging and pots. Slow-growing, requiring only light trimming.
More drought- and frost-tolerant than common laurel, with dark leaves on red stems and fragrant summer flowers. Clips well into formal hedges or standards.
Anise-scented basil with purple stems prized in Asian cooking. Pinch flower spikes to keep leaf production going through summer.
The classic large-leaved Italian basil for pesto. Harvest from the top regularly to encourage a bushy, productive plant.
Bright citrus-scented leaves perfect for fish, salads and teas. Bolts quickly, so harvest often and resow for a steady supply.
Sacred, clove-scented basil used in herbal teas and Thai dishes. Needs warmth and tolerates heat better than sweet basil.
Deep purple foliage makes a striking garnish and pink vinegar. Grow in full sun for the richest colour and pinch out flowers.
Tiny-leaved dwarf basil forming a tidy dome, ideal for pots and windowsills. Naturally compact and slow to flower.
Mexican basil with warm cinnamon aroma and lilac flowers loved by bees. Use fresh in fruit salads, teas and baking.
Huge crinkled leaves big enough to use as wraps. Slightly milder flavour; keep well watered to avoid bitterness.
Classic Italian plum tomato with dense, low-seed flesh ideal for sauces and canning. Tall indeterminate vine, so stake and remove side shoots.
Russian heirloom with dusky maroon, richly flavoured beefsteak fruit. Crops best in a sheltered warm spot or greenhouse.
Popular early Polish cherry tomato bearing heavy trusses of sweet red fruit. Compact and reliable outdoors even in cooler summers.
Beloved Polish raspberry-pink beefsteak with sweet, low-acid flesh. Needs staking and steady moisture to prevent cracking.
Exceptionally sweet orange cherry tomato that crops abundantly until frost. Vigorous indeterminate vine needing tall support.
Famous American heirloom with large, intensely flavoured pink beefsteak fruit. Slower to ripen, so give it the warmest spot.
Determinate paste tomato producing a concentrated crop of meaty plum fruit. Good for processing and needs little staking.
Tangy green-and-yellow striped salad tomato that ripens while staying green. Pick when fruit softens slightly and shows a yellow blush.
Medium-hot chilli giving a heavy crop of thick-walled green pods that redden if left. Crops best under cover in Poland.
Very hot, fruity chilli needing a long warm season to ripen orange. Grow in a greenhouse or polytunnel for a reliable crop.
Spanish tapas pepper eaten young and green, mostly mild with the odd hot one. Pick small and often to keep plants cropping.
Long, tapering sweet Italian pepper that ripens from green to deep red. Superb roasted or grilled; needs warmth and support.
Slender hot red chilli that dries easily for flakes and powder. Productive and tolerant, doing well in pots in a sunny spot.
Fiery Caribbean chilli with intense fruity heat used in jerk cooking. Demands a long, hot season under glass.
Reliable outdoor slicing cucumber with good disease resistance. Pick regularly to keep the vines producing straight, dark fruit.
Heavy-cropping pickling cucumber bearing clusters of small warty fruit. Harvest gherkin-size daily and pickle with dill and garlic.
Frilly red-tinged loose-leaf lettuce picked leaf by leaf as a cut-and-come-again crop. Slow to bolt and decorative in the bed.
Tender lobed loose-leaf lettuce that resists bolting in heat. Sow little and often for continuous picking.
Upright crisp lettuce forming a tall, sweet heart. Keep evenly watered for crunchy, non-bitter leaves.
Hardy winter salad sown in late summer for autumn and early-spring picking. Tolerates frost and crops when little else grows.
Fast peppery salad leaf ready in just a few weeks. Sow in spring and autumn; midsummer crops bolt and turn bitter.
Italian heirloom kale with strappy dark blistered leaves that sweeten after frost. Pick lower leaves and the plant keeps cropping into winter.
Vigorous climbing bean with scarlet flowers loved by bees and long fleshy pods. Give it a tall wigwam and pick pods young and tender.
Italian climbing bean grown for plump red-streaked drying beans. Leave pods to fatten and dry on the plant before shelling.
Hardy bean sown in autumn or early spring for an early summer crop. Pinch out the growing tips once in flower to deter blackfly.
Climbing pea with sweet edible pods eaten whole. Provide netting or twigs to climb and pick often to prolong cropping.
Grown for flat, tender young pods picked before the peas swell. Harvest every couple of days for the sweetest pods.

latin_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra
care_notes: Firm purple-leaved cabbage rich in colour for slaws and braising. A heavy feeder that stores well in a cool cellar.
Hardy crinkle-leaved cabbage that stands through hard frost. The sweetest cabbage for winter cooking; very cold-tolerant.
Fast brassica grown for its crisp swollen stem eaten raw or cooked. Harvest at tennis-ball size before it turns woody.
Sow after midsummer to avoid bolting; forms a crisp upright head for stir-fries and kimchi. Keep well watered and net against pests.
Quick-growing oriental green with crunchy white stems and tender leaves. Best sown in late summer to reduce bolting.
Fast root crop sown in spring and again late summer. Pull at golf-ball to tennis-ball size for sweet, tender roots.
Hardy root vegetable for autumn and winter use, sweeter after frost. Keep soil moist to prevent woody, cracked roots.
Early German variety with firm yellow waxy flesh and good drought tolerance. Reliable in light soils; earth up twice for a clean crop.
Popular Polish very-early variety giving smooth yellow new potatoes. Plant after mid-April and lift young for the best flavour.
Classic floury maincrop ideal for chips, mash and roasting. Stores well; keep tubers earthed up to stop greening.
Hardneck garlic planted in autumn for the largest bulbs; needs winter cold to split into cloves. Snap off the curling scapes to boost bulb size.
Softneck garlic planted in early spring; smaller bulbs but stores longest and can be plaited. Plant as soon as the soil can be worked.
Each set multiplies into a cluster of mild, sweet bulbs. Plant in spring and stop watering once tops yellow, then cure in the sun.
Sweet chestnut-flavoured winter squash with edible skin, no peeling needed. Harvest before frost when the stem corks and store cool.
Sweet nutty squash needing a long warm season to ripen; start indoors. Cure in the sun after harvest for the best flavour and keeping.
Roasted flesh pulls into spaghetti-like strands. Let fruit ripen to deep yellow on the vine before the first frost.
Bushy summer squash with scalloped UFO-shaped fruit. Pick young and often to keep plants productive all summer.
Productive leaf beet with colourful stems, picked leaf by leaf for months. Tolerates light shade and stands into autumn.
Grown for its aniseed-flavoured swollen bulb; sow after midsummer to prevent bolting. Earth up the base for a sweet white bulb.
Lemon-scented thyme superb with fish and chicken. Loves poor, sharp-draining soil and full sun; trim lightly after flowering.
Mat-forming aromatic thyme for paths, gaps in paving and rockeries. Drought-proof once established and a magnet for bees.
The sweet kitchen mint for sauces, teas and mojitos. Always confine in a sunken pot or it will overrun the bed.
Peppermint selection with a chocolate-orange aroma, lovely in desserts and tea. Grow contained as it spreads vigorously.
Clean, intensely minty selection prized for sweet tea. Keep in a pot and cut back hard midsummer for fresh leaves.
The most pungent oregano for pizza and Greek cooking. Grow hard in lean soil and full sun for the strongest flavour.
Sweeter, milder cousin of oregano, grown as an annual in Poland. A staple of Polish cooking; harvest just before flowering to dry.
Ornamental purple-leaved culinary sage that is just as useful in the kitchen. Prune in spring to prevent legginess; very drought-tolerant.
One of the hardiest rosemaries, surviving milder Polish winters with a sheltered spot and sharp drainage. Still safest fleeced or potted in cold areas.
Flat-leaved chives with a mild garlic flavour and white star flowers loved by bees. Cut regularly; divide congested clumps in spring.
Compact lavender with deep violet-blue flowers, the best for low edging and drying. Shear by a third after flowering, never into bare wood.
Early-flowering hardy lavender with soft lavender-blue spikes. Reliable in Poland in lean, alkaline, free-draining soil.
Polish staple grown for its parsnip-like white root used in soups and stocks. Sow early in deep stone-free soil; germination is slow.
Peppery herb traditionally cooked with beans, which it also helps protect from blackfly. Sow direct and harvest before flowering.
Beloved old Polish cooking apple, intensely aromatic and very frost-hardy. Crops heavily; great for purée, pies and storing into winter.
Popular Polish dessert apple with sweet, juicy cream flesh. Reliable cropper; thin fruitlets in June for good size.
Large, sweet-tart Polish apple that stores exceptionally well. Vigorous and productive; pick in October for long keeping.
Crisp, sweet red-striped dessert apple popular worldwide. Needs a pollination partner and June thinning for the best fruit.
Large juicy sweet-sharp apple; a triploid that needs two other varieties to pollinate it. Stores well in a cool cellar.
Hardy North American apple with snow-white flesh slow to brown, great for salads. Reliable in cold gardens.
Reliable, partly self-fertile pear giving long russeted fruit. Pick while still firm and ripen indoors for the best texture.
Popular early Polish pear with sweet, melting juicy flesh. Pick slightly under-ripe as it softens quickly on the tree.
Classic Polish freestone plum, self-fertile and perfect for drying, jam and powidła. Prune only in summer to avoid silver leaf.
Small, intensely sweet golden plums superb for jam and baking. Heavy cropping and self-fertile; net or pick promptly against wasps.
Popular early dark-red sweet cherry needing a pollination partner. Net against birds and prune only in summer to avoid disease.
One of the hardiest peaches, self-fertile and suited to a sheltered sunny Polish wall. Cover in late winter against peach leaf curl.
Classic deep-red preserving strawberry beloved in Poland for jam and freezing. Heavy June cropper; renew beds every three years.
The leading dessert strawberry with large glossy sweet fruit. Mulch with straw to keep berries clean and reduce grey mould.
Day-neutral strawberry fruiting from June until the frosts. Feed regularly and pick often to keep the flushes coming.
Outstanding Polish autumn-fruiting raspberry with large firm sweet berries. Cut all canes to the ground each winter for easy care.
High-yielding summer raspberry with large fruit on spine-free canes. Tie new canes to wires and remove spent ones after harvest.
Sweet golden-fruited autumn raspberry, mild and honeyed. Treat like other primocane types, cutting canes down each winter.
Vigorous spine-free blackberry trained on wires for easy picking. Cut out fruited canes after harvest and tie in the new ones.
The standard reliable blueberry with heavy crops of large mid-season berries. Needs acidic peat soil at pH 4-5 and rainwater.
Extremely hardy early blueberry tolerating heavier, wetter soils than most. Still needs acidic conditions; plant a second variety nearby.
Sweet, mild translucent currant that fruits on older spurs like the red. Tolerates light shade; prune lightly in winter.
Thornless blackcurrant-gooseberry hybrid, vigorous and mildew-resistant. Fruits on young wood, so remove old stems each winter.
Tough, very hardy Polish superfruit grown for antioxidant-rich black berries used in juice and jam. Self-fertile and almost trouble-free.
Extremely hardy berry ripening before strawberries, with a blueberry-raspberry flavour. Plant two varieties for cross-pollination.
Disease-resistant blue wine and dessert grape well suited to the Polish climate. Train on a warm wall and prune hard each winter.
Early-ripening, very hardy white grape that fruits reliably even in cool summers. Excellent for home wine; strict summer pruning needed.
Bushy bedding rose flowering in large clusters from June to frost. Deadhead and prune hard in early spring for the best display.
Trains over arches, walls and pergolas; tie stems horizontally to boost flowering. Prune lightly in late winter, keeping the main framework.
Low spreading, very disease-resistant rose smothering banks in bloom. Almost no pruning needed beyond a tidy-up in spring.
Repeat-flowering shrub rose with full, fragrant blooms in the old-rose style. Feed and deadhead through summer for successive flushes.
Classic large-flowered rose carrying one shapely bloom per stem, ideal for cutting. Prune hard in spring and protect the graft over winter.
Huge white snowball heads on new wood, so prune hard each spring for big flowers. Reliably hardy and shade-tolerant; support floppy stems.
Cone-shaped lime-green flowers ageing to pink, borne on new wood. The toughest, most sun- and cold-tolerant hydrangea; prune in spring.
Large panicles opening white then turning strawberry-pink from the base. Hardy and easy; prune by a third in early spring.
Self-clinging climber smothering shady north walls in lacy white flowers. Slow to establish but trouble-free once away.
Tall, large-flowered tulips that come back for several years, unusual for tulips. Plant in October at three times their depth.
Flamboyant tulips with fringed, twisted, feathered petals. Plant in a sheltered spot as the big blooms catch the wind.
Sturdy mid-season tulips in the widest colour range, excellent for beds and pots. Plant densely in autumn for a bold spring show.
Dwarf early daffodil with several golden flowers per stem, perfect for pots and edging. Naturalises well and is fully hardy.
One of the first flowers of the year, naturalising in lawns and borders. Plant the corms in early autumn for late-winter colour.
Intensely fragrant spring bulb for borders, pots and forcing indoors. Plant in autumn in well-drained soil; handle bulbs with gloves.
Globe-shaped purple flowerheads on tall stems, a magnet for bees. Plant bulbs in autumn among perennials that hide the dying leaves.
The earliest spring bulb, flowering through the last snow. Best moved and divided in the green just after flowering.
Large fully double blooms superb for cutting. Plant tubers after mid-May, stake early and lift before the first hard frost.
Neat little ball-shaped flowers that last well in the vase. Deadhead constantly and lift tubers to store frost-free over winter.
Spiky-petalled blooms in vivid colours for dramatic borders and bouquets. Stake well; the tall stems are brittle in wind.
Large blue-green leaves with wide gold margins lighting up shade. Protect from slugs from the moment shoots appear in spring.
Dark green leaves with crisp white margins for a bright shade accent. Reliable and vigorous; keep soil moist and slug-free.
Giant chartreuse leaves forming a huge mound; one of the more slug-resistant hostas. Takes more sun than most if kept moist.
Vigorous late-flowering clematis smothered in deep purple blooms. A Group 3 type: cut the whole plant back to 30 cm each spring.
Large pale-pink flowers with a carmine bar, flowering early then again in late summer. Group 2: only light pruning above strong buds in spring.
Extremely vigorous spring climber covering walls and trees in scented blooms. Group 1: prune only to keep in bounds, right after flowering.
Spectacular climber dripping with scented blue racemes, needing a strong support. Prune twice yearly, in summer and winter, to force flowering.
Huge fragrant double pink blooms; the classic cut peony. Plant the crown no deeper than 3 cm or it will not flower; support the heavy stems.
Woody peony with enormous silken flowers in late spring. Plant the graft union 10 cm deep and shelter from early-morning sun after frost.
Feathery plumes for damp shade, in pink, red and white. Never let it dry out; the leaves scorch quickly in dry soil.
Fragrant cottage-garden perennial flowering all late summer. Thin the stems in spring and water at the base to dodge mildew.
Upright violet-blue spikes on dark stems, a bee favourite. Cut back after the first flush for a second round of bloom.
Fleshy drought-proof perennial whose pink autumn flowerheads draw butterflies. Leave the seedheads for winter structure and birds.
Grown for year-round coloured foliage, from purple to lime and amber. Lift and replant every few years as the woody crowns rise out of the soil.
Bold spires of pea-flowers in every colour for the early-summer border. Deadhead spent spikes for a second flush and fixes its own nitrogen.
Arching grass with fluffy bottlebrush plumes in late summer. Cut to the ground in early spring before new growth starts.
Strictly upright grass with golden seed plumes that stand all winter. Tough and reliable; cut down in late winter.
Neat blue evergreen tufts for the front of sunny, dry borders and gravel. Comb out dead blades in spring and divide every few years.
Fragrant climbing annual for cutting; the more you pick, the more it flowers. Train up a wigwam or netting and never let it dry out.
Compact free-flowering cosmos blooming all summer in poor soil. Deadhead for endless flowers; too much feeding gives leaves, not blooms.
Tall heat-loving cut flower in vivid colours that bloom until frost. Sow after the soil warms and water at the base to avoid mildew.
Cottage-garden spires of pouched flowers loved by children and bees. Pinch young plants to bush out and deadhead for repeat bloom.
Tough small-flowered viola blooming in cool spring and autumn weather. Deadhead regularly and it can flower through mild winters.
Classic blue meadow annual, easy from a direct autumn or spring sowing. Loved by bees and self-seeds happily for next year.
Fast-growing conifer for tall green hedges, putting on up to 30 cm a year. Trim twice in the growing season and keep well watered when young.
Slim, naturally conical conifer with bright emerald foliage that holds colour all winter. Needs almost no trimming, ideal for narrow screens.
Extremely fast conifer for tall screens, but it needs regular trimming or it gets out of hand. Never cut back into bare brown wood, which will not regrow.
Classic dwarf box for low knot-garden and parterre edging. Clip twice a year and watch closely for box blight and box tree moth.
Compact glossy box that holds good green colour through winter and shapes well into balls. More blight-tolerant than common box.
Box-leaved holly used as a blight-proof substitute for boxwood. Clips into the same neat balls and low hedges and is fully hardy.
The premier formal hedging conifer, dense and long-lived, and unusually it regrows from old wood. All parts are toxic; trim once in late summer.
Deciduous hedging that holds its coppery dead leaves through winter for year-round screening. Tolerates heavy and wet soils; trim in late summer.
Elegant deciduous hedge whose russet winter foliage clings until spring. Needs free-draining soil; trim once in August.
Tough, cheap, fast semi-evergreen hedge that thrives almost anywhere. Trim two or three times a season to keep it dense.
Smothered in golden bells before the leaves in early spring, signalling rose-pruning time. Prune right after flowering, removing a third of old stems.
Deep magenta double flowers with a powerful scent in May. Deadhead spent blooms and prune lightly straight after flowering.
Pure white double florets, intensely fragrant and excellent for cutting. Remove suckers and old wood to keep the bush vigorous.
Compact lilac for small gardens and pots, often reblooming in late summer. Mildew-resistant and needs very little pruning.
Arching shrub hung with ruby trumpet flowers loved by bees in early summer. Prune out a third of the oldest stems after flowering.
Compact shrub with fiery young foliage and pink summer flowers. Cut back hard in early spring for the brightest new leaves.
Cascading shrub draped in white flower clusters in late spring. Prune out a third of the oldest stems right after flowering.
Two-tone cones, white at the tip and pink at the base, flowering on new wood. The hardiest hydrangea group; prune hard in spring.
Evergreen hedge prized for its flame-red young leaves; trim to trigger fresh red flushes. Needs a sheltered spot as hard frost can scorch it.
Evergreen shrub flowering through winter with pink-budded white clusters. Good informal hedging in milder, sheltered Polish gardens.
Native shrub with lacecap white flowers, brilliant red berries and autumn colour. Tough and wildlife-friendly; needs little pruning.
Late-summer flowering shrub with large blue mallow flowers when little else blooms. Slow to leaf out in spring; prune in early spring to shape.
Arching shrub drenched in intensely orange-blossom-scented white flowers in June. Prune out a third of the old flowered stems straight after blooming.
Fast shrub with long deep-purple flower spikes that butterflies adore. Cut back hard to a low framework every spring for the best flowering.
Tough little shrub flowering non-stop from June to autumn in poor, dry soil. Extremely hardy and almost maintenance-free; trim lightly in spring.
Thorny purple-leaved shrub that makes an intruder-proof low hedge with red autumn berries. Very hardy and drought-tolerant; trim to shape in summer.
Grown for vivid red winter stems that glow in low sun. Cut a third of the stems to the base each spring to keep the colour bright.
Bold oak-shaped leaves turning crimson in autumn, with white summer cones. Flowers on old wood, so prune only lightly after flowering.
Elegant small tree with deep red palmate leaves, stunning in a sheltered shady spot. Protect from wind and scorching sun, and never let the roots dry out.
Mound-forming maple with finely dissected lacy leaves, perfect for a large pot or focal point. Shelter from wind and midday sun to prevent leaf scorch.
Spring-flowering small tree with big goblet-shaped pink-white blooms before the leaves. Plant in a spot sheltered from early-morning sun that can spoil frosted buds.
Hardy white-flowered evergreen rhododendron for acidic, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. Mulch with bark and water with rainwater; deadhead after flowering.
Compact evergreen azalea smothered in vivid spring flowers, ideal for acidic shady beds. Keep the shallow roots moist and mulched with leafmould or bark.
Evergreen with fiery red young shoots and drooping white spring flowers. Needs acidic soil and shelter from cold winds that scorch the new growth.
Compact evergreen with red winter flower buds opening to scented spring blooms. Thrives in shade; this male form needs a female plant nearby for berries.
Prized white-variegated form needing bright indirect light to keep its markings. Let the top few centimetres dry between waterings and give it a moss pole.
Heavily white-marbled trailing pothos that needs good light to stay variegated. Very forgiving; let it dry out a little between waterings.
Vivid chartreuse trailing pothos, brightest in good indirect light. Among the easiest houseplants; tolerates occasional neglect.
Upright snake plant with bold yellow leaf margins, nearly indestructible. Water sparingly and never leave it sitting in wet soil, which rots the roots.
Architectural snake plant with stiff round spear-like leaves. Thrives on neglect; let the soil dry out fully between drinks.
Statement plant with huge violin-shaped leaves that hates being moved or overwatered. Give it bright light, steady warmth and water only when the top soil dries.
Trailing philodendron with green leaves splashed lime and gold. Easy-going in indirect light; pinch the tips to keep it bushy.
Dramatic round leaves with purple undersides that fold up at night. Demands high humidity, warmth and rainwater, or the leaf edges brown.
Glossy houseplant with long-lasting waxy red spathes almost year-round. Likes warmth, bright indirect light and a slightly moist, airy compost.
Ribbed French beefsteak with rich flavour, semi-determinate so needs only light staking. Crops well outdoors in a warm sheltered spot.
Early striped salad tomato with red-and-orange marbled fruit. Reliable and tangy; stake and remove side shoots.
Hugely popular sweet cherry tomato carrying long trusses of bite-sized fruit. Vigorous and dependable even in cool summers.
Classic blocky sweet bell ripening from green to red. Reliable under cover; support the heavy fruiting stems.
Medium-hot waxy yellow pepper that reddens when fully ripe, great for frying. Crops earlier than most chillies, even outdoors in a warm year.
Bright red, juicy sweet-sharp apple that keeps until spring in store. Heavy and regular cropper; thin in June for size.
Hardy autumn apple with soft sweet aromatic flesh, popular in Polish orchards. Eat soon after picking as it does not store long.
Very early, heavy-cropping strawberry with firm bright fruit. Hardy and reliable; mulch with straw to keep berries clean.
Low evergreen creeper for permanently moist, acidic, peaty soil. Very hardy; mulch with sand and never let it dry out.
Robust perennial-type basil with camphor-clove scent and purple-veined leaves loved by bees. Grow from cuttings and overwinter on a windowsill.
Aromatic semi-evergreen herb with blue flower spikes that draw bees. Drought-proof in lean soil; trim after flowering to keep it neat.
Delicate aniseed herb that bolts in heat, so sow in spring and autumn shade. Cut young leaves often and keep the soil moist.
Cucumber-flavoured herb with edible blue star flowers that bees adore. Self-seeds freely; let a few plants run to seed for next year.
Tender herb with intensely sweet calorie-free leaves used as a sugar substitute. Grow in a pot and overwinter frost-free indoors.
Long-lived perennial cropping for twenty years from a permanent bed. Do not cut spears in the first two years; stop harvesting by late June.
Hardy perennial pulled for tangy spring stems; never harvest the toxic leaves. Stop pulling in midsummer to let the crown recover.
Vigorous perennial grown for its pungent root, a Polish Easter staple. Plant in a bucket or buried container as it spreads aggressively.
Tall sunflower relative grown for knobbly tubers lifted through winter. Plant where it can be contained, as any missed tuber regrows.
Heat-loving crop that needs a greenhouse or polytunnel in Poland. Pick the pods young at 8-10 cm before they turn fibrous.
Climbing wax bean with tender, stringless golden pods over a long season. Give it a wigwam and pick every few days to keep it cropping.
Striking lime-green brassica with fractal spiralling heads, milder than cauliflower. Needs rich soil and unbroken steady growth for good heads.
Mild peppery Japanese salad leaf, fast and ideal for cut-and-come-again. Best in spring and autumn as it bolts in summer heat.
Hardy perennial leaf with a sharp lemony tang, a Polish soup classic. Pick young leaves and remove flower spikes to keep them tender.
Grown for crunchy leaf stalks; demands constant moisture and rich soil. Modern self-blanching types save the old trench-and-earth-up work.
Sturdy prairie perennial with large purple-pink daisies on strong stems. Drought-proof once established; leave the seedheads for goldfinches.
Golden black-centred daisies smothering the plant from midsummer to autumn. Reliable and long-flowering; divide every few years.
Clouds of late daisies feeding butterflies and bees before winter. Divide clumps every two years to control mildew and keep them vigorous.
The classic autumn flower carrying the season to the first hard frosts. Pinch out the tips in early summer for a bushy, flower-laden plant.
Sun-loving perennial with sculptural early-summer flowers in every colour. Plant the rhizome half-exposed and bake it in full sun, or it will not flower.
Tough, almost indestructible perennial throwing up golden trumpets all summer. Each flower lasts a day but the show runs for months; deadhead for neatness.
Carpeting perennial flowering violet-blue non-stop from June to frost. Brilliant weed-smothering ground cover; shear over once midseason to refresh.
Stately spires of bell flowers beloved by bees, thriving in dappled shade. A biennial that self-seeds; all parts are poisonous, so site with care.
Airy see-through perennial topped with purple flower clusters that butterflies mob. Self-seeds gently; leave stems over winter and cut back in spring.
Easy edible-flowered annual that self-seeds and flowers for months. A classic companion plant; deadhead for non-stop bloom or let it set seed.
Iconic scarlet meadow annual, sown direct as it hates root disturbance. Scatter seed on bare soil in autumn or spring and let it self-sow.
Towering cottage-garden spires of saucer flowers, classic against a sunny wall. A short-lived biennial that self-seeds; watch for rust on the leaves.
